5	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
6	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
7	
8	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
9	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
10	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
11	\[
12	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
13	  \qquad
14	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
15	\]
16	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
17	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
18	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
19	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
20	
21	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
22	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
23	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
24	% groups)
25	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
26	% SOURCE QUOTE:
27	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
28	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
29	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
30	% abelian groups defined by the formula
31	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
32	% \end{dfn}
33	%  [\dots]
34	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
35	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
36	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
37	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
38	% topologies  by
39	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
40	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
41	%  \end{dfn}"
42	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
43	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
44	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
45	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
46	homomorphisms.}
47	
48	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
49	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
50	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
51	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
52	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
53	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
54	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
55	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
56	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
57	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
58	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
59	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
60	structure. This is the content of
61	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
62	
63	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
64	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
65	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
66	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
67	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
68	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
69	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
70	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
71	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
72	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
73	\begin{enumerate}
74	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
75	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
76	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
77	    isomorphism classes;
78	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
79	    for \(\otimes\);
80	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
81	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
82	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
83	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
84	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
85	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
86	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
87	\end{enumerate}
88	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
89	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
90	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
91	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
92	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
93	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
94	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
95	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
96	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
97	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
98	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
99	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
100	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
101	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
102	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
103	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
104	supplied.
105	
106	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
107	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
108	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
109	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
110	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
111	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
112	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
113	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
114	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
115	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
116	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
117	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
118	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
119	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
120	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
121	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
122	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
123	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
124	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
125	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
126	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
127	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
128	avoids. The
129	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
130	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
131	The whole \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / stalk
132	apparatus is therefore vestigial for the group law and off the critical path.
133

142	iso-classes never consumes a coherent monoidal category, and the associator is

144	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance on which route~(e) hinges is never required.

241	\paragraph{The gap and route~(e), superseded.} The substrate
242	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) of this chapter is the
243	sheafification of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\), and
244	sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the localization
245	\(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\). Route~(e)
246	proposed to obtain a \emph{full} monoidal structure on
247	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)

255	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}); it never invokes
256	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), a coherent monoidal category, or the stalkwise
257	whiskering apparatus. The route~(e) instance is therefore vestigial and off the
258	critical path.

261	reference only, since that route is superseded. The \emph{stalk module structure}
262	is already present in Mathlib: the file
263	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk} supplies, for a topological

266	\(\mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) of a presheaf of modules \(M\),
267	together with the germ--scalar compatibility \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\); a linearity
268	packaging of the induced stalk map of a morphism was built project-side
269	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, the \emph{linearity half} of ingredient (d.1)).
270	Route~(e) additionally required a (d.1)-\emph{bridge} between the site-level
271	\(J.W\) and the topological stalkwise-isomorphism criterion on
272	\(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), and (d.2) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
273	\otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk with the relative module-presheaf tensor; there is
274	moreover no monoidal \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) in Mathlib. Because the group law
275	is realized through the direct-gluing associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso},
276	via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) and never through
277	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), none of this stalk apparatus is on the critical path;
278	it is retained only to document the superseded route.

281	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
282	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
283	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
284	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the

288	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
289	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
290	
291	\begin{definition}
292	
293	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
294	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
295	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
296	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
297	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
298	  \[
299	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
300	  \]
301	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
302	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
303	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
304	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
305	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
306	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
307	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf

376	\begin{remark}
377	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
378	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
379	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
380	        lem:whisker_of_W}
381	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
382	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
383	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
384	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
385	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
386	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
387	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
388	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
389	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
390	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
391	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
392	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
393	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
394	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
395	
396	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
397	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
398	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
399	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
400	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
401	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
402	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
403	  fixed-base presheaves in
404	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
405	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
406	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
407	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). But this
408	  apparatus is not on the critical path at all: the group law is realized without
409	  it.
410	
411	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
412	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
413	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
414	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
415	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
416	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
417	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
418	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
419	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
420	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
421	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
422	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
423	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
424	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
425	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
426	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
427	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
428	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
429	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
430	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
431	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
432	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
433	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
434	\end{remark}

436	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
437	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
438	
439	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
440	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
441	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
442	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
443	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
444	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
445	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
446	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
447	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
448	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
449	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
450	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
451	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
452	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
453	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
454	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
455	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
456	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
457	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
458	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
459	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
460	to the off-path route~(e).

848	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
849	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
850	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
851	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
852	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
853	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
854	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
855	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
856	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
857	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
858	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
859	\end{lemma}

861	\begin{proof}
862	
863	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
864	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
865	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
866	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
867	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
868	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
869	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
870	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
871	\end{proof}

895	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
896	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
897	  left-whiskered morphism
898	  \[

900	  \]

902	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
903	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism

930	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for

932	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
933	  \[

935	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).

1419	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W,lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} and the sheafification
1420	  bridge \cref{lem:isiso_sheafification_map_of_W}); through
1421	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} --- whose left-whiskering component is
1422	  itself unproved --- it transitively depends on an open proof obligation and is
1423	  therefore \emph{not} axiom-clean. The gluing route described below is the
1424	  \emph{intended} obligation-free realization, but discharging it requires
1425	  \emph{morphism-level descent for \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\)}: gluing a family of
1426	  local isomorphisms, agreeing on overlaps, into a single global isomorphism of
1427	  sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. That is the same descent-infrastructure family
1428	  the \(\otimes\)-inverse needs (\cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}); accordingly, the
1429	  associator re-route \emph{and} the deletion of the vestigial whiskering/stalk
1430	  apparatus are deferred jointly with the dual block, and are not available with the
1431	  current infrastructure. The gluing description below records the target realization.
1432	
1433	  The isomorphism is built by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through the
1434	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, with no
1435	  recourse to any whiskering or stalk apparatus. Fix a
1436	  common open cover \(\{U\}\) of \(X\). On each member \(U\) form the canonical
1437	  composite
1438	  \[
1439	    \bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P\bigr)\big|_U

1440	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1441	    \bigl(M|_U \otimes_U N|_U\bigr) \otimes_U P|_U
1442	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1443	    M|_U \otimes_U \bigl(N|_U \otimes_U P|_U\bigr)
1444	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1445	    \bigl(M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\big|_U ,
1446	  \]
1447	  in which:
1448	  \begin{itemize}
1449	    \item the first arrow is two applications of the restriction-compatibility
1450	      isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} along the open immersion
1451	      \(U \hookrightarrow X\): first \((M \otimes_X N)|_U \cong M|_U \otimes_U
1452	      N|_U\) tensored with \(P|_U\), then \(\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X
1453	      P\bigr)|_U \cong (M \otimes_X N)|_U \otimes_U P|_U\);
1454	    \item the middle arrow is the \emph{canonical presheaf-level associator}
1455	      \(\alpha\) of the (symmetric) monoidal category
1456	      \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\)
1457	      (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\),
1458	      \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)), read on the restricted modules;
1459	    \item the third arrow is again two applications of
1460	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, in the opposite direction.
1461	  \end{itemize}
1462	  Each of the three arrows is a \emph{canonical} isomorphism: the
1463	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is induced
1464	  by the open immersion with no choices, hence natural in its module arguments, and
1465	  the presheaf associator \(\alpha\) is a fixed natural transformation. Therefore,
1466	  on a double overlap \(U \cap U'\), restricting the \(U\)-composite and the
1467	  \(U'\)-composite to \(U \cap U'\) yields the same morphism: each of the three
1468	  arrows commutes with the further restriction to \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1469	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the presheaf associator, so the two local
1470	  composites agree on the overlap.
1471	
1472	  Because the internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(-,-)\) of sheaves of
1473	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is itself a sheaf on \(X\), a family of morphisms defined
1474	  on the members of an open cover and agreeing on the overlaps glues to a single
1475	  global morphism. Applying this both to the local composites above and to their
1476	  local inverses produces a global morphism
1477	  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \to M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\) whose restriction
1478	  to each \(U\) is the local composite, together with a two-sided inverse glued from
1479	  the local inverses; hence the glued morphism is a global isomorphism --- the
1480	  associator. The single non-formal input of this re-route is the
1481	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the presheaf
1482	  associator (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)) and the sheaf-of-modules
1483	  internal-hom gluing are already available.
1484	
1485	  Note that, unlike the pointwise-existence statement
1486	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial} --- a \emph{proposition} proved
1487	  cover-pointwise with \emph{no} overlap obligation --- the associator is
1488	  \emph{global data}, a single isomorphism of sheaves on all of \(X\). Assembling it
1489	  from local pieces is therefore genuinely stronger than that pattern: the
1490	  overlap-naturality compatibility of the local composites must be discharged before
1491	  gluing, and is discharged above. The locally-trivial hypotheses of the statement
1492	  are not consumed; the comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is applied to the
1493	  global, untrivialised restrictions \((M \otimes_X N)|_U\), so no free-cover
1494	  specialisation can replace it. The vestigial
1495	  whiskering / \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / stalk apparatus
1496	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W,lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W,lem:jw_ismonoidal}) plays
1497	  no part in this proof and is superseded. The group law on \(\otimes\)-iso-classes
1498	  consumes only the \emph{existence} of the resulting isomorphism, never its pentagon,
1499	  triangle, or naturality coherence.
1500	\end{proof}

2514	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
2515	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
2516	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
2517	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
2518	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
2519	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
2520	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
2521	  \[
2522	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
2523	  \]
2524	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
2525	  abelian-group structure with addition
2526	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
2527	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
2528	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
2529	  inverse
2530	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
2531	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
2532	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2533	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
2534	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
2535	  map
2536	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
2537	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
2538	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
2539	\end{theorem}

2541	\begin{proof}
2542	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
2543	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
2544	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2545	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
2546	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
2547	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
2548	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
2549	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
2550	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
2551	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
2552	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
2553	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
2554	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
2555	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
2556	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the
2557	  commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes}
2558	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}): multiplication
2559	  \([L]\cdot[L'] = [L \otimes_{C\times_k T} L']\) is well-defined and
2560	  commutative-monoidal by the coherence isomorphisms
2561	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
2562	  and an iso-class is a unit precisely when its carrier is
2563	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse
2564	  being carried by the predicate. Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the
2565	  existence of an isomorphism --- so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon,
2566	  triangle, hexagon) is invoked; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on
2567	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the additive form of this units group. This mirrors
2568	  Mathlib's own Picard group: \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its
2569	  \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\),
2570	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) transported from
2571	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\); here
2572	  the same \(\mathrm{Units}(\cdots)\) shape is transported to the scheme-level
2573	  \(\otimes_X\). (The scheme-level Picard group is genuinely absent from
2574	  Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup} records ``connect to
2575	  invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project supplies it lightly.)
2576	  The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
2577	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the scheme-level analogue of
2578	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\); the geometric predicate
2579	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) is retained separately and is connected to
2580	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) only by an off-critical-path equivalence (only the
2581	  geometric \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)-style
2582	  direction is nontrivial, and it is not needed here).
2583	
2584	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
2585	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
2586	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
2587	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
2588	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
2589	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
2590	
2591	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
2592	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
2593	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
2594	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
2595	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
2596	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
2597	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
2598	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
2599	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
2600	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
2601	\end{proof}
